question: How does the NuScale single control room concept differ from conventional multi-unit plants?

nuscale_ch01_0372.title: SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3: Control Room Area Ventilation SystemII.4 Control Room
nuscale_ch01_0372.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3 requirements for the Control Room Area Ventilation System. It confirms compliance with criteria related to protection against natural phenomena, environmental effects, and control room sharing, noting that up to 12 modules share a single control room. For radioactive material release, the system does not use emergency filtration but instead supplies clean air from compressed air tanks to maintain control room habitability during accidents.

nuscale_ch01_0370.title: SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3: Control Room Area Ventilation SystemII.2 Environmental and Dynamic Effects
nuscale_ch01_0370.summary: The section confirms that the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report meets the regulatory criteria outlined in SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3, specifically regarding environmental and dynamic effects on the Control Room Area Ventilation System. It states that the design conforms to these requirements, with no exceptions or additional comments noted for this criterion.

nuscale_ch01_0864.title: Nuclear Power Plants to be Operated on Multi-Unit Sites
nuscale_ch01_0864.summary: This section outlines requirements for COL applicants referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification to assess and manage potential hazards from construction activities at multi-unit sites. The evaluation must ensure that safety-related structures, systems, and components of both existing and new units are protected, and that operational safety limits are not exceeded. These requirements do not apply to construction activities at individual NuScale Power Plants with operating modules.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate the need for external power and large coolant loops, significantly lowering the risk of core damage and improving safety compared to traditional nuclear plants.

nuscale_ch01_0402.title: DSRS 9.5.2, Rev 0: Communication SystemsII.11 Compliance with GDC 19
nuscale_ch01_0402.summary: This section explains that the NuScale design departs from GDC 19 by supporting an exemption and instead complies with a NuScale-specific principal design criterion (PDC-19). The design ensures that communication equipment is available in the control room to support all normal and emergency operations, including intra-plant and off-site communications, even during equipment failures or power loss. Additionally, certain regulatory requirements (10 CFR 73.45) are not applicable to NuScale, as it does not reprocess spent fuel or handle special nuclear material.

nuscale_ch01_0023.title: Containment Systems
nuscale_ch01_0023.summary: The NuScale containment vessel (CNV) is maintained under vacuum during normal operations, which reduces moisture and corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits oxygen for safety. In the event of an emergency, passive cooling rapidly reduces pressure and temperature, and the plant’s design—featuring passive safety systems and a small fuel inventory—results in improved safety and a lower core damage frequency compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0111.title: Figure 1.7-2: Instrumentatio n and Controls Symbol Legend
nuscale_ch01_0111.summary: This section provides a legend explaining the symbols used for instrumentation and controls in NuScale’s safety analysis diagrams. It defines the logic gate functions, control room indicators, switch types, and other symbols relevant to system status and operations, ensuring clear interpretation of the diagrams. Additionally, it includes notes on piping and instrumentation diagram conventions, such as vent and drain valve representations.

nuscale_ch01_0865.title: Nuclear Power Plants to be Operated on Multi-Unit Sites
nuscale_ch01_0865.summary: This section outlines requirements for COL applicants referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification to assess and manage potential hazards from construction activities at multi-unit sites. The evaluation must ensure that safety-related structures, systems, and components of both existing and new units are protected, and that operational safety limits are not exceeded. These requirements do not apply to construction activities at a single NuScale plant with operating modules.

nuscale_ch01_0066.title: Material Referenced
nuscale_ch01_0066.summary: This section lists the topical and technical reports incorporated by reference in the NuScale Power Plant Design Certification Application, as detailed in Tables 1.6-1 and 1.6-2. These reports cover various aspects such as quality assurance, risk significance, safety classification, accident analysis methodologies, and fuel evaluation methods, and are referenced throughout specific sections of the Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR).

nuscale_ch01_0852.title: SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0: Crediting Manual Operator Actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) AnalysesC.1.B Review Criteria for Phase 1 (Analysis)
nuscale_ch01_0852.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) analyses, as outlined in SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0. It confirms that NuScale meets the review criteria for all phases of D3 analysis, with SRP Appendix 18-A superseding previous guidance, while also noting that certain aspects of probabilistic risk assessment remain the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0014.title: Acronyms and Abbreviations
nuscale_ch01_0014.summary: This section explains the conventions used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) for numbering and organizing text, tables, figures, and pages, as well as the handling of proprietary and sensitive information. It clarifies that sensitive material is withheld from the public version in accordance with regulatory requirements, while the public version excludes such content.

nuscale_ch01_0853.title: SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0: Crediting Manual Operator Actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) AnalysesC.2.B Review Criteria for Phase 2 (Preliminary Validation)
nuscale_ch01_0853.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) analyses, as outlined in SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0. It confirms that NuScale meets the review criteria for all phases (analysis, preliminary validation, integrated system validation, and long-term integrity), superseding previous guidance, while noting that certain aspects of probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) remain the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0050.title: Diesel Generator Buildings
nuscale_ch01_0050.summary: The Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs) in the NuScale Power Plant each house a single backup diesel generator and their auxiliary equipment, serving as support structures without any safety-related systems or functions supporting engineered safety features (ESFs). The DGBs contain diesel engines, generators, HVAC systems, and maintenance areas.

nuscale_ch01_0851.title: SRP 18.0, Rev 2: Human Factors EngineeringII.C Review of the HFE Aspects of Modifications Affecting Risk Important Human Actions
nuscale_ch01_0851.summary: This section states that the SRP 18.0, Rev 2 criteria for reviewing human factors engineering (HFE) aspects of modifications affecting risk-important human actions are not applicable to NuScale, as they pertain to existing reactor licensees. However, NuScale conforms to the review criteria in SRP Appendix 18-A for crediting manual operator actions in diversity and defense-in-depth (D3) analyses, while site-specific hazards and PRA updates are the responsibility of the COL applicant under SRP 19.0.

nuscale_ch01_0164.title: SRP 6.4, Rev 3: Control Room Habitability SystemII.7 Toxic Gas Hazards
nuscale_ch01_0164.summary: The NuScale control room habitability system does not rely on emergency ventilation but instead uses compressed air tanks to provide clean air during accidents, eliminating the risk of toxic gas or radioactive material entering through ventilation inlets. The guidance on toxic gas hazards is only partially applicable, as programmatic requirements are the responsibility of the COL applicant. Additionally, certain regulatory criteria related to air filtration and containment spray systems are not applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0161.title: SRP 6.4, Rev 3: Control Room Habitability SystemII.5 Relative Location of Source and Control Room
nuscale_ch01_0161.summary: The NuScale design does not rely on traditional control room emergency ventilation systems for habitability during accidents; instead, it uses compressed air tanks to supply clean air, preventing the entry of radioactive or toxic materials. As a result, several regulatory criteria related to ventilation systems and ESF filtration are not applicable, though some dose guidelines and toxic gas hazard requirements are partially addressed or deferred to the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0049.title: Central Utility Building
nuscale_ch01_0049.summary: The Central Utility Building (CUB) is a non-safety-related structure that houses essential utility services for the plant, such as chiller equipment, air systems, water treatment, maintenance, and security functions. Additionally, the plant includes Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs) for backup power, and its design incorporates human factors engineering to minimize errors and optimize control and automation.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It references tables and figures that list and explain the symbols and diagrams used, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0068.title: Electrical and Instrumentation and Control Drawings
nuscale_ch01_0068.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation, and control (I&C) drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It references tables and figures that list and explain the symbols used in these diagrams, and notes that applicants referencing the NuScale design must provide additional site-specific diagrams and legends as needed.

nuscale_ch01_0048.title: Security Buildings
nuscale_ch01_0048.summary: The Security Buildings (SCBs) at a NuScale Power Plant are nonsafety-related structures that include the primary access control building. These buildings support plant security functions but are not classified as safety-related, distinguishing them from other critical plant infrastructure.

nuscale_ch01_0061.title: Control Rod Drive Mechanism Proof Test
nuscale_ch01_0061.summary: The NuScale Integral System Test Program uses the NIST-1 facility, a scaled model of the NuScale Power Module (NPM), to generate thermal-hydraulic data for system characterization and validation of safety analysis codes such as NRELAP5 and PIM. These tests ensure that the unique features and safety methodologies of the NPM are accurately represented and validated, supporting the design certification process.

nuscale_ch01_0062.title: Control Rod Assembly Drop and Control Rod Drive Shaft Alignment Test
nuscale_ch01_0062.summary: This section describes the testing and validation of the NuScale control rod assembly (CRA) drop and drive shaft alignment, including remote connect/disconnect operations. The tests, conducted at Curtiss Wright facilities, demonstrated reliable and repeatable engagement and disengagement of the CRA, informing design improvements but not establishing the final design basis. The NPM features longer, remotely disconnectable drive shafts with multiple alignment supports to ensure proper operation.
